behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local backoff math/state progression (consecutive failure count -> delay, clamped by max). No behavior text requires module handoff, real HTTP, OS timing primitives, or subprocesses. Higher levels were considered and rejected: the text is purely about delay calculation ("doubles... up to a configured maximum"), which disqualifies System/Agentic/Workflow and does not require Integration wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff policy function/object with deterministic inputs: failure counts 1..N and maxDelay. Assert delay sequence (e.g., 100, 200, 400, ...), and assert clamping at configured max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is a module-boundary handoff: HTTP response classification (rate-limit detection) must feed retry/backoff computation. A unit test of only classifier or only backoff can miss contract mismatch between modules. Integration brake checks: no real OS/network/subprocess is required because stubbed responses can reproduce 429 + headers; no AI/ML dependency; no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry controller + response parser + backoff policy with a stub transport returning a rate-limit response (e.g., 429 and retry metadata). Assert the controller flags it as retryable and computes the expected next delay.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears when timer/scheduler, retry orchestrator, and transport interact: request must be replayed after delay and result surfaced unchanged. A single-function unit test cannot validate resend + transparent return path. Higher levels rejected: behavior text does not require real network/OS primitives; a fake clock and stub transport can expose this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock + stub transport: first call returns rate-limit, second call returns success payload. Invoke `call()` once, advance virtual time past computed delay, assert same request was resent and final resolved value equals second response payload.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior combines retry-attempt accounting with terminal error mapping. Real failure boundary is orchestration state (attempt count) handed to error-construction path. Unit-only error-type mapping could pass while retry loop never reaches mapping correctly. Higher levels rejected: no need for real HTTP/OS/subprocess; deterministic stubbed failures are sufficient; no AI/ML content dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Configure maxRetries=N. Stub transport to always return rate-limit/failure. Execute `call()` and assert exactly N retries were attempted and rejection is `QuotaExhaustedError` (not generic Error / transport error type).

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is end-to-end within local library modules: retry loop control + backoff state + quota tracker interaction. Infinite-loop bugs typically occur at these internal handoffs, not in one isolated function. Integration brake checks: no explicit requirement for real network/OS/subprocess; fake clock and bounded stub responses can detect non-termination. Not Agentic/Workflow because no LLM steps.
behaviors.test_description: Run client with fake clock and scripted stub responses that never recover. Assert termination within bounded virtual time/attempts and that error surfaces once limits are reached; fail test if pending promise or attempts exceed bound.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets shared state across concurrent callers ("same process"), a cross-module concurrency boundary (public API -> shared quota manager). Unit tests of quota math alone can miss per-call-site isolation bugs. Higher level rejection: phrase "same process" disqualifies need for distributed/system infra; in-process concurrent async tasks reproduce the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client, launch multiple concurrent `call()` invocations with controlled stub transport and deterministic quota limits. Assert aggregate consumption reflects one shared bucket (not per-caller buckets) and blocking/retry decisions are coordinated across callers.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: FIFO dispatch is a queueing/orchestration property across request intake, waiting queue, and dispatcher modules. A local unit test of queue data structure alone can miss integration bugs in enqueue/dequeue timing under async retry/wait logic. Higher levels rejected: behavior does not require real OS/network; in-process scheduler + stub transport suffices; no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple calls in known order while quota forces waiting. With fake clock and stub transport, release capacity stepwise and assert outbound dispatch order matches submission order exactly.
